chapter_title: Chapter 5: Legal Principles of an Insurance Contract

questions.text: The Latin term 'Uberrima Fides' means:
questions.explanation: 'Uberrima Fides' translates to 'Utmost Good Faith'. It is the foundational principle of all insurance contracts, requiring both parties to be completely honest.

questions.text: Which of the following principles ensures that a person cannot insure a stranger's life or property?
questions.explanation: Insurable Interest means you must suffer a direct financial loss if the insured person dies or the property is destroyed. Without it, you cannot insure a stranger.

questions.text: In Life Insurance, when must 'Insurable Interest' exist?
options: Only at the time of claim/death
Only at the time of taking the policy (inception)
Both at the time of taking the policy and at the time of death
It is not required in life insurance
questions.explanation: Crucial Exam Rule: In Life Insurance, insurable interest must exist ONLY at the time of taking the policy. It is not legally required to exist at the time of death (e.g., a divorced couple where the policy was taken during marriage).

questions.text: In Marine Insurance, when must 'Insurable Interest' exist?
options: Only at the time of taking the policy
Only at the time of the loss
Both at inception and at the time of loss
Only at the time of premium payment
questions.explanation: In Marine Insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time the loss occurs. The goods may be bought and sold while in transit.

questions.text: In Fire Insurance, when must 'Insurable Interest' exist?
options: Only at the time of taking the policy
Only at the time of the loss
Both at the time of taking the policy AND at the time of loss
questions.explanation: In Fire/General Insurance, you must own or have a financial interest in the property BOTH when you buy the policy and when the fire occurs.

questions.text: What is a 'Material Fact' in an insurance contract?
options: Any fact that the agent asks about
A fact that would influence the insurer's decision to accept the risk or set the premium
A fact related to the physical material of the property
A fact that is available on public records
questions.explanation: A material fact is any crucial piece of information (like a pre-existing heart condition) that would help the underwriter decide whether to accept the risk and at what price.

questions.text: The principle of 'Indemnity' means:
options: To make a profit from an insurance claim
To compensate the insured and restore them to the same financial position as before the loss
To pay the maximum sum assured regardless of the loss
To punish the person who caused the accident
questions.explanation: Indemnity ensures the insured does not make a profit out of a loss. They are only compensated for the actual financial loss suffered.

questions.text: Which of the following types of insurance does NOT strictly follow the Principle of Indemnity?
options: Life Insurance and Personal Accident Insurance
questions.explanation: Life and Personal Accident insurance are exceptions to Indemnity because human life cannot be valued in money. The insurer pays a fixed pre-agreed amount (Sum Assured).

questions.text: If a person hides a pre-existing illness like Diabetes while filling the life insurance proposal form, it is a breach of:
questions.explanation: Hiding or misrepresenting material medical facts is called non-disclosure, which is a direct violation of the Principle of Utmost Good Faith.

questions.text: Which principle allows the insurance company to step into the shoes of the insured to recover the claim amount from a guilty third party?
questions.explanation: Subrogation is the transfer of rights. After paying the claim, the insurer gets the legal right to sue the third party who actually caused the accident.

questions.text: The principle of Subrogation does NOT apply to:
questions.explanation: Since Subrogation is a corollary (sub-part) of Indemnity, and Indemnity does not apply to Life Insurance, Subrogation also does not apply to Life Insurance.

questions.text: If a factory owner takes two fire insurance policies from two different companies for the same factory, which principle prevents him from claiming the full amount from both companies and making a double profit?
questions.explanation: The Principle of Contribution ensures that if there are multiple policies, all insurers will share the actual loss proportionately. The insured cannot claim more than the actual loss.

questions.text: What does 'Causa Proxima' or Proximate Cause mean?
options: The most distant cause of a loss
The active, efficient, and direct cause that sets in motion a chain of events leading to the loss
A cause that is completely unrelated to the accident
The cause determined by the police
questions.explanation: When there are multiple causes for a loss, the insurer looks for the Proximate Cause (the most direct, dominant, and effective cause) to see if it is covered under the policy.

questions.text: An individual has an 'unlimited' insurable interest in whose life?
options: Their own life and their spouse's life
questions.explanation: By law, a person is assumed to have an unlimited insurable interest in their own life and in the life of their legally wedded spouse.

questions.text: Does a creditor (lender) have an insurable interest in the life of a debtor (borrower)?
options: No, there is no blood relationship
Yes, but only up to the extent of the outstanding loan amount
Only if the debtor agrees in writing
questions.explanation: A creditor has a financial interest in the debtor staying alive to repay the loan. Therefore, they have an insurable interest, but only up to the amount of the debt.

questions.text: An insurance contract entered into without 'Insurable Interest' is considered legally:
options: A wagering (gambling) contract and is null and void
Valid if the premium is paid
questions.explanation: Without insurable interest, taking an insurance policy is legally considered gambling (a wagering contract) and is completely void in the eyes of the law.

questions.text: The duty to disclose material facts under the principle of Utmost Good Faith applies to:
options: Both the proposer and the insurance company
questions.explanation: Utmost Good Faith is a mutual duty. The proposer must disclose all medical/personal facts, and the insurer must transparently disclose all policy terms and conditions.

questions.text: Which of the following facts is NOT required to be disclosed by the proposer?
options: Facts that are common knowledge or matters of public law
questions.explanation: A proposer is not required to disclose facts that lessen the risk, facts that the insurer is expected to know (common knowledge), or matters of law.

questions.text: A life insurance policy was taken by a husband on his wife. Later they got divorced, and the wife passed away. Will the death claim be payable to the ex-husband?
options: No, because the insurable interest was lost after divorce
Yes, because in life insurance, insurable interest is only required at the time of taking the policy
The premium will be refunded, but no claim paid
questions.explanation: In life insurance, insurable interest must exist at inception. The subsequent loss of insurable interest (due to divorce) does not invalidate a previously valid policy.

questions.text: Subrogation and Contribution are corollaries (extensions) of which main legal principle?
questions.explanation: Both Subrogation and Contribution exist specifically to support the Principle of Indemnity, ensuring that the insured does not make a profit from multiple sources.

questions.text: An employer taking a 'Keyman Insurance' policy on their top performing manager is an example of which principle?
questions.explanation: The employer has a direct financial interest in the life of the key manager because their death would cause a financial loss to the business. This creates an Insurable Interest.

questions.text: In case of a breach of Utmost Good Faith by the policyholder, the insurance contract becomes:
options: Voidable at the option of the insurer
Valid but with a penalty fee
questions.explanation: If a material fact is concealed, the contract becomes 'voidable'. The insurance company has the legal right to cancel the policy and reject claims.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes 'Concealment' in insurance?
options: Failing to volunteer a material fact that ought to be disclosed
Making a mistake in the address
questions.explanation: Concealment is the passive failure to reveal a material fact, even if a direct question wasn't asked. It is a breach of utmost good faith.

questions.text: A house is insured against fire. It is damaged by water used by firefighters to extinguish a fire in the neighboring house. What is the proximate cause of the water damage?
questions.explanation: The fire was the direct, active, and efficient cause that set the chain of events into motion (calling firemen -> spraying water). Therefore, Fire is the proximate cause, and the claim is payable.

questions.text: The concept that 'Insurance is not a contract to make money, but a contract to make good the financial loss' best defines:
questions.explanation: The core purpose of Indemnity is to put the insured back in the same financial position they were in just before the loss, preventing any financial gain.
